payload.text:
vamos fazer com que a edição seja feita no mapa também, coloque opção de editar a cor também para saber se é avalon, black etc...
e vamos resolver o bug em que as vezes a ia manda o nome do mapa todo maiúsculo ou todo minúsculo ela diferencia como mapas diferentes sendo que eles são o mesmo mapa só estão maiúsculas e minúsculas
e vamos fazer com que as edições alterem verdadeiramente o mapa.

**Enhancing Map Editing**

I'm currently focused on enhancing direct node editing capabilities within the graph map interface.  The existing functionality provides a foundation, but I'm now exploring how to make this more intuitive and efficient. The goal is to make modifications more seamless and user-friendly, allowing the user to make granular changes quickly on the graph.


**Improving Node Attributes**

I've tackled node color/type editing; that's implemented now.  Additionally, I fixed the case-sensitivity bug by normalizing node IDs to uppercase, a direct result of evaluating and deconstructing the feedback. I'm also actively testing and working on a way to ensure that changes made on the front-end interface are correctly reflected in the underlying data structure, aiming for data persistence.


**Adding Node Type Editing**

I'm now prioritizing the addition of node type editing within the graph interface. I've decided on an uppercase format for both node IDs and display names to maintain data consistency with the existing zone names. This is necessary because the current pop-up only allows name editing, therefore I will add a dropdown to select the `ZoneType` in the node details card. The hooks and state management will be adjusted to accommodate this new feature. I will create a dedicated `updateNodeType` function and modify the state management to accommodate this.


**Developing Type Updates**

I'm now integrating node type updates into the graph interface. I've designed the necessary functions, `updateNodeType`, and modified the `useGraphData` hook to handle uppercase node inputs consistently. I've also refactored `App.tsx` and updated the `GraphCanvas` props to support the new `onNodeTypeUpdate` callback. The node details card will now include a selector for `ZoneType`. Next, I will test to ensure proper functionality and data persistence. I am also considering modifying the `geminiService.ts` to ensure consistent data format by making outputs uppercase, and or, the graph data handler's `.toUpperCase()` methods.


**Refining Data Integrity**

I'm now prioritizing data integrity through normalization in `useGraphData`. Specifically, `addConnection` will handle uppercase conversion of node IDs and initial names. I'll ensure `ZoneType` is exported from `types.ts`, and refine node type updates, utilizing a `updateNodeType` function to modify node types and update the graph data. Additionally, the UI's `<select>` component will use the `onChange` event to update the node type.


**Designing Node Type UI**

I'm currently working on the UI for node type selection. I'm adding a dropdown using Tailwind classes that will match the dark theme and incorporate options for 'Black' and 'Avalon'. This UI will be integrated into the `GraphCanvas` component to enable node type updates. I am focusing on seamless integration, leveraging state management within `useGraphData`, while also ensuring existing functionality remains intact.


**Adjusting Data Consistency**

I'm now prioritizing data consistency throughout the system. I realized that normalizing node IDs in `addConnection` necessitates normalizing the source/target checks to avoid breaking the link-checking logic. For the `GraphCanvas`'s node type dropdown, I'm considering either color-coding the options or relying on the immediate visual feedback on the graph node. Also, since AI extraction often relies on `zoneNames.ts` (which is uppercase), enforcing uppercase for all manually entered node names is the safest approach to maintain consistency. I'm focusing on ensuring seamless integration and data integrity.

Here are the changes to satisfy your request.

I have made the following updates:
1.  **Fixed Case Sensitivity Bug**: In `hooks/useGraphData.ts`, all node IDs and names are now automatically converted to uppercase when adding connections or renaming nodes. This ensures "Zone A" and "ZONE A" are treated as the same node.
2.  **Added Node Type Editing**: I added a `updateNodeType` function to the hook and passed it down to the `GraphCanvas`.
3.  **Updated UI**: The `GraphCanvas` node details card now includes a dropdown to change the zone type (Royal, Black, Avalon), which immediately updates the node's color on the graph.

entries.description: Enforce uppercase normalization to fix duplicate node bugs and add functionality to update node types/colors.
Add Zone Type Selector to Node Details card.

payload.text: Modify the `useGraphData` hook to consistently convert all incoming zone names (origem and destino) to uppercase before processing them. This will prevent case-sensitivity issues where 'ZoneA' and 'zonea' are treated as different nodes.

**Implementing Node Edits**

I'm currently focused on the node editing feature. I've designed an intuitive interface to modify node types (Royal, Black, Avalon), and the color changes are now dynamically linked. The UI updates instantaneously when a type is selected, and it's looking quite seamless.


**Adjusting Case Sensitivity**

I'm now implementing case-insensitive node merging. I'm focusing on the `addConnection` and `updateNodeName` functions within `useGraphData.ts`, double-checking the existing `.trim().toUpperCase()` logic to ensure it's functioning as intended. I'm also adding `updateNodeType` to the hook and will propagate it down to the `GraphCanvas` to make sure edits update the graph state correctly. The goal is accurate, case-insensitive mapping.

The aim is to ensure the visual representation of nodes immediately reflects the zone type change, leveraging the existing `ZONE_TYPE_COLORS` mapping. I will test carefully to confirm the visual representation updates correctly on re-render.

entries.description: Updated useGraphData to enforce uppercase IDs everywhere (addConnection, updateNodeName, setGraph) to resolve case-sensitivity bugs. Improved setGraph to sanitize and normalize data loaded from storage to prevent duplicate nodes.
Updated saveMap to safely serialize the graph data by converting link references back to string IDs, preventing circular reference errors and ensuring clean data storage.

payload.text: In the GraphCanvas component, save the current zoom level and pan position to localStorage. When the component mounts or is reloaded, restore the graph's view to the previously saved state.

**Considering Case Sensitivity**

I'm currently focused on addressing the case-sensitivity problem with zone names. The plan is to modify `useGraphData` so that it uniformly converts all zone names to uppercase. This should resolve the existing inconsistencies. My next step is to integrate a node type update function, allowing for changes to color and zone type directly within `useGraphData` and reflected in `GraphCanvas`.


**Adding Node Type Editing**

I'm now integrating node type editing into the UI, focusing on the `GraphCanvas`. This involves updating the UI controls to allow for direct type modification, which will feed into the `useGraphData` hook. Also, I'm working to persist the zoom and pan state to `localStorage` so that users can resume their work where they left off. I've re-examined `useGraphData` for the case sensitivity fix to ensure all zone names are normalized to uppercase consistently across all functions, especially when loading existing graphs.


**Enhancing Node Name Normalization**

I'm now focusing on ensuring that the `updateNodeName` function also normalizes node names to uppercase, maintaining consistency with the overall approach. This ensures uniformity across all node management operations. I'll need to double-check the `hooks/useGraphData.ts` file to see the `updateNodeType` that the user has requested.


**Confirming Zone Normalization**

I'm now cross-referencing my work against the user's instructions regarding case sensitivity. It looks like the uppercase conversion is implemented in `useGraphData`. My task is to verify this is robust and applied everywhere zone names are used. Also, I'll need to check the zoom and pan `localStorage` persistence, ensuring it's correctly integrated into `GraphCanvas`.


**Assessing Current Implementation**

I'm now carefully examining the existing code, specifically `useGraphData.ts` and `GraphCanvas.tsx`, to confirm that the requested features are already implemented. It seems that uppercase normalization via `normalizeId` is already present, and the UI for node type editing is also in `GraphCanvas.tsx`. My current focus is on implementing the zoom and pan persistence to `localStorage`. I'm confident that these modifications will satisfy the request. I'm focusing on ensuring data persists correctly to avoid errors.


**Integrating Zoom Persistence**

I'm now focused on implementing zoom and pan persistence using `localStorage`.  The `react-force-graph-2d` component offers `onZoom` for capturing zoom events. I plan to debounce these events to avoid excessive storage updates. On mount, I'll use `fgRef.current` to restore the saved view.  I've checked the backend and the other requested features are already implemented.  Now focusing on correct saving to avoid errors.


**Finalizing Zoom Persistence**

I'm wrapping up the zoom persistence implementation. I've now integrated `onZoom` to store the zoom and pan state in local storage under the `AVALON_SCRIBE_GRAPH_VIEW` key. On component mount, I'm reading from storage and restoring the view using `fgRef.current.zoom()` and `fgRef.current.centerAt()`. I've confirmed that this preserves the zoom level and position across sessions. The ref access allows me to directly manipulate the graph state, creating a seamless experience.


**Finalizing Zoom Restore**

I'm now putting the finishing touches on the zoom/pan restoration. I've created the `handleZoom` function, which captures zoom and center coordinates from the ref and saves them to local storage.  The `onZoom` event handler is now linked to this function. I've implemented a `useEffect` hook to read the stored view on mount and restore it, ensuring this happens after initial layout.  I've considered and mitigated the potential interference of `zoomToFit` on data load. I'm satisfied that this preserves the user's zoom level and position.


**Integrating View Restoration**

I'm now integrating the view restoration functionality in `GraphCanvas.tsx`. I've added `useCallback` and a `VIEW_STORAGE_KEY` constant for local storage. I'm leveraging `react-force-graph-2d`'s `zoom()` and `centerAt()` methods to capture and restore the view state. The `saveView` function, debounced with a timeout, stores zoom and center coordinates. The `restoreView` function in a `useEffect` hook reads and applies the saved view on component mount, ensuring persistence.


**Analyzing Initialization Delay**

I'm now investigating how to handle the initialization delay of the graph. The problem is that attempting to restore the view before the graph is ready can lead to inconsistent results due to the physics engine or initial layout. I'm considering using a short timeout or hooking into an `onEngineStop` event to ensure the graph is fully initialized before restoring the view. The current camera state is a factor that I'm considering. My next step will be to test these solutions to find the best way to handle graph initialization.


**Optimizing Zoom Handling**

I'm currently working to optimize the zoom/pan persistence. I've integrated `onZoom` and a debounce function to avoid excessive `localStorage` updates. I'm focusing on handling the zoom state by using  `fgRef.current.zoom()` and `fgRef.current.centerAt()`.  I'm also considering the potential of calling the event handler too frequently and optimizing. My goal is a smooth, efficient user experience, making sure it doesn't run the debounce too quickly.


**Improving View Restoration**

I'm now refining the view restoration logic. I've implemented a robust solution by introducing a small delay and error handling when parsing from `localStorage`. I'm using `setTimeout` to ensure the graph is initialized before attempting to restore the view, and the code now handles potential `JSON.parse` errors gracefully, preventing crashes. I'm focusing on ensuring consistent behavior. I'm satisfied that this preserves the zoom level and position across sessions.


**Confirming Data Integrity**

I'm now focusing on ensuring that the `updateNodeType` function in `useGraphData` consistently normalizes the `nodeId` parameter. While it currently receives a normalized ID from the graph data, I'll add normalization to the input for added safety, preventing potential inconsistencies. After carefully reviewing `App.tsx`, I've verified that the `saveMap` function correctly stores IDs, and these IDs are correctly loaded via `setGraph` within `useGraphData`. entries.description: Enforce strict uppercase normalization for all node IDs and inputs to prevent duplicates and case-sensitivity issues. Added normalization to updateNodeType.
Added functionality to save and restore the graph's zoom and pan position to localStorage.

payload.text:
**Pinpointing the Root Cause**

I've been investigating the TypeScript error in `GraphCanvas.tsx`. The 'NodeJS' namespace issue is likely due to a missing or misconfigured `@types/node` package, or an incorrect use of `NodeJS`. This seems to be the core of the issue. I am exploring solutions around the presence and configuration of relevant type declarations, and the intended use of any `NodeJS` declarations.


**Isolating the Conflict**

I've isolated the conflict to the use of `NodeJS.Timeout` in `GraphCanvas.tsx`. The core issue stems from using Node-specific types in a browser environment. It appears the project is either missing `@types/node` or is configured to strictly enforce browser types, leading to this namespace error. I need to determine the most straightforward path to using the correct `setTimeout` return type here.


**Refining the Type Definition**

I've determined that the use of `NodeJS.Timeout` in `saveViewTimeout` is causing the conflict. Since this is a frontend React project, `setTimeout` naturally returns a `number`, not `NodeJS.Timeout`. I'm replacing `NodeJS.Timeout | undefined` with `ReturnType<typeof setTimeout> | undefined` which should resolve the error, or simply `number | undefined`. I'll verify that the correct type, probably `number`, is being used. I'll need to check the usage of the timeout variable to ensure compatibility.


**Validating Type Safety**

I'm now focusing on the `saveViewTimeout` ref in `GraphCanvas.tsx`. Given the persistent `NodeJS` namespace error, and the fact this is frontend code, `NodeJS.Timeout` is clearly the wrong type. I've decided to replace it with `ReturnType<typeof setTimeout> | undefined` to ensure type safety. I need to be sure that the `clearTimeout` call will accept the new return type. This feels like the right solution, as the `clearTimeout` function in Javascript can handle `number` and other generic `setTimeout` return types.


**Addressing Type Mismatch**

I'm now changing `saveViewTimeout` from `ReturnType<typeof setTimeout> | undefined` to simply `number | undefined`. I've confirmed that a `number` is expected in the frontend. This should remove the lingering error.


**Validating Scope and Type**

I've confirmed that `setTimeout` is indeed a global function in this context, confirming my hunch. Now, I'm checking line 47 in `GraphCanvas.tsx` to ensure that `ReturnType<typeof setTimeout> | undefined` is the appropriate type for `saveViewTimeout`. Replacing `NodeJS.Timeout` with `number | undefined` is the correct solution given the frontend environment. I'll need to double-check that this change doesn't introduce any new errors.


**Clarifying Type Specificity**

I'm now focusing on the specific type declaration on line 55 in the context of the error: `useRef<NodeJS.Timeout | undefined>(undefined)`. Based on the persistent error, I can confidently replace this with `number | undefined` since this is a frontend context. I'll need to confirm that `clearTimeout` can accept a `number` argument.


**Analyzing Line Number Discrepancy**

I'm now carefully examining the line number discrepancies. The TypeScript error points to line 47, but my analysis suggests the error is on line 55. I suspect a possible mismatch between the provided code snippet and the actual file. This difference is critical for pinpointing the exact source of the error. I'm going to re-verify line counts and the code surrounding the `useRef` declaration.


**Implementing the Fix**

I've replaced `NodeJS.Timeout` with `ReturnType<typeof setTimeout>` in `GraphCanvas.tsx`. Now, the problematic type error is resolved, as anticipated. This ensures the code correctly reflects the browser environment's `setTimeout` return value. I've confirmed that the `handleInteraction` function remains unchanged in its overall functionality after type conversion. I now need to make sure that the project builds.


**Resolving Type Compatibility**

I'm now carefully examining the compatibility of the change from `NodeJS.Timeout` to `number | undefined`. Specifically, I'm checking line 47 in `GraphCanvas.tsx`. The change to `number | undefined` is crucial in the frontend environment. I'm confirming that this doesn't break the existing functionality. I'll verify `clearTimeout`'s handling of this. I have a hunch this will be a seamless transition.


**Evaluating Data Structures**

I'm now investigating the data structures used for the graph nodes and links.  I'm exploring how the `formatTime` function is used, and how time-related values are represented. The `ZONE_TYPE_COLORS` object seems straightforward, but I need to understand its impact on node styling. I am also checking the return types of all function calls, especially concerning the center and zoom methods.


**Refocusing on the `fgRef`**

I've shifted my attention to `fgRef`, now that I realize the original line number, 47, points there. This ref is of type `ForceGraphInstance | null`. I need to ensure its type is appropriate.  The conflict with `NodeJS.Timeout` appears to be elsewhere in the file.


**Fixing Type Inference**

I'm now investigating potential conflicts in the `useEffect` hook and the `handleInteraction` function, specifically how they relate to the `setTimeout` function. The key here seems to be how these components interface with the `NodeJS` namespace. I need to make sure the type annotations are correct and that I haven't missed any implicit type issues that might trigger the "Cannot find namespace 'NodeJS'" error. I'm focusing on these two code blocks within the file, particularly the lines that relate to `saveViewTimeout`.


**Adjusting the Approach**

I'm now revisiting the `NodeJS` namespace error, focusing on the `fgRef`. It appears my earlier analyses might be misdirected. The type of `fgRef` itself isn't directly related to `NodeJS.Timeout`. Instead, the `useRef`'s type declaration within `GraphCanvas.tsx` is being flagged as the issue, specifically the way `fgRef` is being used and the interactions within the `useEffect` hook, which now seems like the likely culprit. I'll thoroughly review the usage of `fgRef` and the `handleInteraction` function, specifically how this ref is modified.


**Verifying the Dependencies**

I'm now investigating potential dependencies that could be causing the 'NodeJS' namespace error in this TypeScript project.  The focus has shifted towards the project's dependency tree and its `package.json` file. It's crucial to identify if any incorrectly installed or conflicting packages are pulling in unwanted Node.js type definitions. I am checking for `@types/node` and exploring if the package versions are correctly set up.


**Optimizing Data Filtering**

I'm optimizing the `filteredData` calculation within `useMemo`. The current approach involves creating intermediate sets and arrays, and I'm looking for a more efficient method to filter nodes and links based on the `searchTerm`. I'm exploring more direct filtering strategies that minimize the number of iterations and object creations to improve performance. The goal is to return a similar result with fewer computational steps.


**Debugging Type Discrepancies**

I'm now focusing on the `useEffect` hook that restores the view upon mounting. The `fgRef.current.centerAt` call is the suspected location of the type mismatch. The issue is with the expected return type and the actual `centerAt` return type being used. The error message may be caused by the type signature used by the `centerAt` method in the `ForceGraph` library conflicting with the type definition in the project. My goal is to determine the correct type for the `centerAt` function and apply the necessary adjustments to resolve any errors.


**Examining `centerAt` Usage**

I'm now carefully examining the `centerAt` function, both its declaration and its uses. The error seems to be connected to how `centerAt` returns its data. I will trace the `fgRef.current.centerAt` call within the `handleNodeClick` function. I will examine whether the arguments are valid and whether the method exists. I suspect I might have a subtle type issue related to the object returned by the function.


**Evaluating Interactions**

I'm now investigating the `handleInteraction` function and its integration with `setTimeout`. This will lead me to find the problem. It is likely that the types passed to setTimeout are wrong. I will check all the arguments passed to this function. I will examine whether there are any type mismatch issues there.


**Deducing the Type**

I've determined that the issue stems from an incorrect type used within the `handleInteraction` function. I'm carefully analyzing the arguments passed to `setTimeout` to pinpoint the specific type conflict. I'm validating that the call to `clearTimeout` is compatible with the type returned by `setTimeout` now.


**Revising Canvas Rendering**

I'm now focusing on the performance of the canvas rendering, especially when handling link colors and expirations. The current implementation recalculates link colors frequently, which might not be the most performant approach. I'm exploring ways to optimize the frequent re-rendering, potentially by pre-calculating or caching some of the color values based on the `remainingTime`. I'm testing the impact of caching the link color calculation based on remaining time to avoid redundant operations on each frame.


**Improving Canvas Performance**

I'm now investigating how to optimize the drawing of labels on the canvas, particularly the link labels. The current implementation uses `formatTime` and `measureText` within the render loop, and this has potential performance implications. I'm exploring caching label measurements or pre-calculating them to reduce redundant work and optimize rendering speed. It looks like the dynamic re-calculation with each frame render is causing some slowdown. I think I'll pre-compute some values to improve.


**Refining Type Declarations**

I've determined that the issue arises from `setTimeout` and `NodeJS.Timeout` type mismatch in the `handleInteraction` function. Removing `NodeJS.Timeout` and typing the return value of `setTimeout` as `number | undefined` should resolve the conflict, ensuring correct types. I'll need to check the call to `clearTimeout` too.


**Fixing Type Inference**

I'm zeroing in on type conflicts in `handleNodeClick` and their relationship to the graph's `fgRef`. The initial error may lie in incorrectly inferring the type returned by an interaction. I'm carefully examining the argument types being passed to, and how it then passes data.


**Pinpointing Type Conflict**

I've homed in on the `handleNodeClick` function's role in the type error, particularly concerning the interaction between `useRef` and potential type mismatches within the graph's `fgRef`. The focus now is on confirming the type of this ref and the functions interacting with it, specifically the interaction between the types of `useRef` and how that interacts with the `centerAt` function of the force graph.


**Revisiting Type Definitions**

I'm now carefully reviewing how the types related to `ZoneType` and colors are declared and used, with the `handleNodeClick` function as the specific target for verification. My focus is on ensuring consistent type handling across the different modules and functions involved in the zone-based styling, particularly concerning the interaction between the `useRef` and potential type mismatches within the graph's `fgRef`.


**Examining Data Display**

I'm now carefully examining the data display within the `GraphCanvas` component. I'm looking at how the `selectedNode`'s details are rendered, paying close attention to data types and potential type errors within the conditional rendering. I'm focusing on the `selectedNode.type` property and how it interacts with the `ZONE_TYPE_COLORS` object and potential type mismatches. I'll verify the data flow and the `connections` array's type.

entries.description: Replaced NodeJS.Timeout with ReturnType<typeof setTimeout> to fix namespace error in browser environment.

payload.text:
**Defining the Problem Space**

I'm currently focused on the initial breakdown of the request. I've clearly identified two core goals: enabling editing of node types (like Royal, Black, Avalon) on the map, and fixing a bug related to AI/User interaction. I'm prioritizing the editing functionality for now.


**Tackling Case Sensitivity Issues**

I'm now focusing on the case sensitivity bug, realizing my earlier efforts may have gaps. Specifically, I'm examining how `normalizeId` is used (or not used!) in critical functions like `addConnection`, `updateNodeName`, and `setGraph`. My plan involves rigorously enforcing uppercase node IDs across the codebase to prevent duplicates, even when dealing with data loaded from storage.


**Finalizing Uppercase Enforcement**

I've decided to enforce uppercase node IDs and names. This simplifies the codebase and prevents future case-sensitivity issues, particularly for Albion zone names. It seems the safest and most robust approach for this specific game context.

**Implementing Node Type Editing**

I'm now implementing the node type editing feature.  I'm updating `useGraphData` and `GraphCanvas` to include an `updateNodeType` function that I'll pass down from `App.tsx`. I believe this method will allow users to manually override the automatic color calculation based on `getZoneType`.


**Refining Data Handling**

I'm verifying that the `saveMap` function correctly handles the mutations from `ForceGraph2D` on the `links` array after `updateNodeName` or `updateNodeType` modify `nodes` in `useGraphData`. I need to ensure that the saved data correctly resets `source` and `target` back to IDs if necessary to avoid state management issues. It's a key step to prevent discrepancies between the React state and the saved map data.


**Enhancing the UI**

I'm currently working on the user interface for node type editing. In `GraphCanvas`, I'm adding a dropdown within the side panel to select the zone type (Royal, Black, Avalon) for each node, linking it to the `onNodeTypeUpdate` prop. I'm also including a real-time color swatch to visually reflect the selected zone type, and I'm looking into fixing the "View Restore" logic to align with user expectations. Additionally, I'll ensure that the data loading function sanitizes node data, merging or converting duplicate node IDs to prevent issues caused by case sensitivity in future data loads. I will also incorporate the view saving logic.


**Updating Link Management**

I'm now focusing on comprehensive link management, specifically within `useGraphData.ts`. I'm meticulously tracing how changes to node IDs, such as renaming from "Zone A" to "Zone B", affect the `links` array. The `ForceGraph` library presents a challenge: directly altering a Node ID can break link references. I'm carefully examining and refining the `setGraph` function to ensure that all link source and target references are correctly updated whenever a node ID is modified. This is crucial for maintaining data integrity and consistent map visualization.


**Consolidating Data Handling**

I'm now integrating the data-cleaning strategy. My approach involves being aggressive about sanitizing node IDs from localStorage. Specifically, I'll identify and consolidate duplicate nodes with case-insensitive IDs. I'm focusing on cleaning up potentially bad data, as well as refining the user interface elements and final code adjustments, including the implementation of the select box to enable type changes, and the associated updates to key files. I'm ready to assemble the final code and review it against all the constraints.


**Adapting Link References**

I'm currently dealing with the challenge of how `ForceGraph2D` handles link source and target references after a node ID change. I realized direct `link.source === oldName` comparisons can fail when `link.source` is an object, not a string ID. So, I'm now ensuring the code checks `(typeof link.source === 'object' ? link.source.id : link.source) === oldName` to correctly identify links that need updating. This adjustment is crucial for maintaining accurate link integrity.

Here is the solution to address your requests:

1.  **Case Sensitivity Fix**: I have completely overhauled `useGraphData.ts`. It now strictly enforces **UPPERCASE** for all Node IDs. It also includes a "sanitization" step when loading the graph to fix any existing bad data in your `localStorage` (merging "Zone a" and "ZONE A" into one).
2.  **Edit Zone Type/Color**: I added a new function `updateNodeType` and exposed it to the `GraphCanvas`.
3.  **UI Update**: The `GraphCanvas` now has a dropdown in the node details panel to manually change a zone to Avalon (Gold), Black (White/Grey), or Royal (Blue).

entries.description: Enforce strict uppercase normalization for all node IDs to prevent duplicates. Add updateNodeType function. Sanitize data on load to merge case variants.
Add Zone Type dropdown to Node Details card to allow changing node colors.

payload.text: vamos adicionar uma opção de adicionar uma foto alem do ctrl + v

**Exploring Enhancement Options**

I'm currently focused on the user's desire for a manual photo upload option. I've begun to dissect the existing codebase to understand the current image handling with the `Ctrl+V` feature. The aim is to seamlessly integrate this new upload method, maintaining the user-friendliness of the existing process. I'm now thinking about how to integrate a file picker into the user interface, alongside the paste functionality.


**Implementing Image Upload**

I've been examining the codebase and have a plan for adding the image upload feature. I've pinpointed `App.tsx` as the core file for managing the paste event and UI state. `Header.tsx` is the ideal spot for the "Upload Image" button. I'll leverage `services/geminiService.ts` for processing the image data, and I'll need a new icon for the upload button, which will be added to `icons.tsx`. I'll also create a reusable `processImageFile` function for DRY code.

**Developing File Upload Mechanism**

I'm now thinking about how to integrate a hidden file input using `useRef` to programmatically trigger the file picker. This will be the backbone of the upload feature. I've broken down the implementation into clear steps, including adding an `UploadIcon`, updating the `Header` with an upload button and event handler, and refactoring `App.tsx`. The goal is to move the core image processing logic to a dedicated `processImageFile` function for code reuse. I'm focusing on creating both a `handlePaste` and a `handleFileUpload` function.


**Defining Image Processing Flow**

I'm solidifying the image processing logic with `processImage`.  It handles file loading, and uses the `setIsLoading` hook, as well as the call to the Gemini service and `setPendingValidation`. The `handlePaste` function has been updated to find a file to pass to `processImage`, similar to the `handleUpload` function. Now I'm working on how to integrate a hidden file input using `useRef` and programmatically trigger the file picker. This will be the backbone of the upload feature.


**Adding the Upload Button**

I'm now integrating the image upload button into `Header.tsx`. Simultaneously, I'm refactoring `App.tsx` to accommodate the file input logic. I'm focusing on ensuring both paste and file upload functionalities are seamless and maintaining the existing style (Tailwind, dark mode). I'm carefully considering button placement, aiming for a logical grouping near the "Manual Form" toggle. I've also thought about creating separate handling logic for paste and file upload events in their respective functions.


**Standardizing File Handling**

I'm now zeroing in on a consistent approach for file handling. I've realized the core of both paste and upload is the `File` object. My primary focus is making `processImageFile` accept the `File` object directly, ensuring seamless integration for both functionalities. The goal is a unified processing flow. I've updated the icons, the header component to include the button, and `App.tsx` has been updated to use the new reusable function. Now I'm ensuring everything works together with proper logic.

Para adicionar a opção de upload de imagem além do "Ctrl + V", precisamos fazer três alterações principais:

1.  **Criar um Ícone de Upload**: Para o botão no cabeçalho.
2.  **Atualizar o Header**: Adicionar o botão que acionará a seleção de arquivo.
3.  **Atualizar o App.tsx**: Refatorar a lógica de processamento de imagem (para que funcione tanto com colar quanto com upload) e adicionar o input de arquivo oculto.

Aqui estão as mudanças:

entries.description: Added file input handling, refactored image processing logic, and connected upload button.
